name: trading_intelligence
title: Trading Intelligence Evaluation Rubric
description: Senior trading-analyst rubric for judging the intelligence-generation process (discovery, research, business value) of a Newsfind run. Scores are 0-5 per category. Weights are configurable; layer weights and within-layer category weights are normalized at runtime.
persona: A senior trading analyst at a professional trading organization (energy/commodity trading firm, quant fund, asset manager, or market intelligence desk).

layers.name: Information Discovery
layers.description: Quality of information acquisition. The system's most important capability: discovering, acquiring, and validating information from the internet. A system that writes well on incomplete information must score lower here than one that discovers superior information.

categories.name: Primary Source Discovery
categories.description: Can the system locate the original source (regulatory filings, company announcements, grid/TSO publications, government agencies, exchange notices, port authorities, official reports, valuable/official regional social accounts) rather than news aggregators, blogs, or secondary reporting?

categories.name: Information Latency
categories.description: How close is the discovered information to the original publication event? Higher scores for surfacing information nearer to source-publication time (e.g. original 08:00 vs Reuters 08:12 vs later media).

categories.name: Source Coverage
categories.description: Did the system explore a sufficiently broad information space across categories: official institutions, regulatory databases, industry organizations, regional media, specialist publications, academic sources, corporate communications, technical reports, official regional social sources?

categories.name: Non-Obvious Source Discovery
categories.description: Can the system identify sources not commonly found by generic search (terminal operator reports, shipping authority data, infrastructure operator notices, local government disclosures, industry datasets) rather than only Reuters/Bloomberg?

categories.name: Source Authority Assessment
categories.description: Can the system correctly distinguish information producers, information consumers, commentators, and aggregators?

layers.name: Research Quality
layers.description: How well information is transformed into intelligence.

categories.name: Entity Discovery
categories.description: Did the system identify relevant entities (companies, assets, infrastructure, commodities, countries, regulatory bodies, key individuals)?

categories.name: Relationship Discovery
categories.description: Did the system identify relationships between entities (supply-chain, infrastructure, ownership, market, regulatory dependencies)?

categories.name: Causal Reasoning
categories.description: Did the system explain why something happened, what caused it, and the likely consequences?

categories.name: Research Depth
categories.description: Did the system move beyond surface reporting: multiple independent sources, cross-validation, supporting evidence, historical context?

categories.name: Signal-to-Noise Ratio
categories.description: Did the report focus on important information while minimizing irrelevant detail?

layers.name: Trading Intelligence
layers.description: Business value to professional market participants.

categories.name: Market Relevance
categories.description: Is the information relevant to market participants?

categories.name: Actionability
categories.description: Can a trader, analyst, or risk manager take action based on the report?

categories.name: Potential Market Impact
categories.description: Could the discovered information affect prices, volatility, supply, demand, or risk?

categories.name: Information Edge
categories.description: Did the system provide information, relationships, or insights not obvious to generic LLMs or standard news-consumption workflows? One of the most important metrics.
